nativeRange.habitat: Lagoons and seaward reefs from the surface down to roughly 50–60 m, with juveniles favoring isolated patches of branching coral or rubble in shallow sandy areas and adults occurring singly or in pairs on the slopes of deep lagoon or outer reefs.

decorPreferences: robust, well-secured live rock with caves and overhangs
no fragile decor — the fish will rearrange anything moveable
no exposed glass heaters, airline tubing, or unsecured equipment
sand substrate it can sift and blow

diet.notes: A robust benthic-invertebrate feeder. In the wild it crushes sea urchins, hard-shelled molluscs, crustaceans, tube worms, and pieces of branching coral, and is one of the few fish documented preying on crown-of-thorns starfish. In aquaria, a varied meaty diet is generally advised — krill, squid, clams, mussels, whole shrimp, chopped fish, and sea urchins. Hard-shelled items are commonly recommended several times per week to wear down the continuously growing teeth.

summary: The largest of the Indo-Pacific triggerfishes (reaching ~75 cm) and one of the most aggressive aquarium fish in the trade. Generally not reef-safe, demands an extremely large fish-only or FOWLR tank, and is notorious for biting hands and rearranging decor.
careNotes:
The Titan Triggerfish (Balistoides viridescens) is the largest triggerfish in the Indo-Pacific, with FishBase reporting up to 75 cm total length; LiveAquaria lists 30 in (~76 cm) as the maximum captive size. Most aquarium specimens settle in around 40–55 cm but should be planned for as if they will reach the full 75 cm. Lifespans are not tightly documented, but FishBase's resilience figures (population doubling time 4.5–14 years) and the species' size are consistent with 10–20 years in well-kept systems.

Tank requirements are extreme. LiveAquaria recommends a minimum of 300 US gallons (~1136 L); given the species' adult size, very active swimming, strong territoriality, and habit of redecorating the rockwork, AquaMate biases the published minimum sharply upward to 2000 L (~525 US gal) with a tank footprint of at least 3 m so the fish has genuine swimming room as an adult. Smaller volumes are kept successfully short-term, but tend to amplify aggression and to leave no margin once the fish hits its growth potential.

Water chemistry is standard tropical reef: roughly 23–27°C, pH 8.0–8.4, SG 1.021–1.025, dKH 8–12. The species is hardy and generally tolerant of minor parameter swings once acclimated.

Titan Triggerfish are not considered reef-safe. They actively crush and consume sea urchins, snails, crustaceans, tube worms, and corals, and will nip or destroy sessile inverts even when well fed. They are also infamous for biting heaters, powerheads, airline tubing, and the keeper's hands — wild Titans are widely reported to attack divers, especially females guarding a benthic nest, and the same intensity translates to maintenance work in captivity. Keepers are commonly advised to use long tongs and never to place bare hands in the tank.

Temperament is firmly aggressive. Only one specimen should be kept per system, and tankmates must be similarly large, robust, and assertive — large angelfish, moray eels, large wrasses, size-matched lionfish, and groupers are commonly cited as workable companions. Small, slow, peaceful, or shy species, other triggerfish, and any crustaceans or echinoderms should be avoided.

Diet should be varied, protein-rich, and include regular hard-shelled foods (whole shrimp, clams in shell, mussels, occasional sea urchins) to wear down the continuously growing teeth — overgrown teeth can prevent feeding and become a husbandry emergency.

Notable traits the schema cannot capture directly: a Mustache Triggerfish marking (origin of one of its common names); a dorsal trigger spine that locks erect into rockwork for shelter; ciguatera-carrier status in some wild populations (a human food-safety concern, not an aquarium-tank toxicity issue); IUCN status Least Concern (2022). All aquarium-trade specimens are wild-caught from the Indo-Pacific.

Cross-source notes — Adult size: LiveAquaria 30 in (76 cm), FishBase 75.0 cm TL, Wikipedia up to 75 cm; agreed. Tank size: LiveAquaria 300 gal (~1136 L) — bias-larger pick of 2000 L applied per AquaMate tank-size rule given the 75 cm adult, aggressive territoriality, and redecorating behavior. Temperature: LiveAquaria 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), FishBase 25–29°C (mean 28.1°C) — averaged to 23–27°C. Care level: LiveAquaria 'Easy', but rated 'advanced' here to reflect the tank-volume requirement, aggression, dental-maintenance needs, and handling hazards.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is essentially unreported. In the wild, Titan Triggerfish are demersal nesters: a female digs a shallow funnel-shaped nest in sand, lays eggs there, and aggressively guards the nest — biting divers who stray into the conical territory above it. Spawning occurs in roughly monthly cycles around the full or new moon depending on the population. Reproduction in aquaria is considered effectively impossible given the tank volumes and pair-bond dynamics involved.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Species detail page (entered from the Triggerfish collection root https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/triggerfish supplied by the discovery manifest). Provided minimum tank (300 gal), care level (easy), temperament (aggressive), reef compatibility (no), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.0–8.4, SG 1.021–1.025, dKH 8–12), maximum size (30 in), diet, family (Balistidae), and behavioral notes (rearranges decor, hostile to divers, mustache marking).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name (Bloch & Schneider, 1801), taxonomy (Balistidae / Tetraodontiformes), max length 75.0 cm TL, wild temperature 25–29°C (mean 28.1°C), depth 0–60 m, Indo-Pacific distribution (Red Sea to Tuamotu), trophic level 3.3 ± 0.44, resilience suggesting population doubling time 4.5–14 years, hostile/territorial behavior toward divers when nest-guarding, and IUCN status Least Concern (Jan 2022).

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Tertiary corroboration only (not on the source whitelist for husbandry — used because designated secondaries BlueZoo Aquatics, Reef2Reef wiki, Fishkeeping World, and TFH Magazine all returned 403/404 or expired certs for this species). Confirmed max size 75 cm, depth to 50 m, Indo-Pacific range (absent from Hawaii), diet (urchins, molluscs, crustaceans, tube worms, coral, crown-of-thorns starfish), monthly lunar-tied spawning, dorsal-spine threat displays, and that bites are non-venomous but mechanically dangerous.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive-breeding records found. Demersal nest spawner in the wild, with monthly lunar-tied reproduction; aquarium reproduction is effectively unreported given tank-size and pair-dynamics demands.
